{{Short description|Studies into the ties between these aspects}}
'''Intelligence and personality''' have traditionally been studied as separate entities in [[psychology]], but more recent work has increasingly challenged this view. An increasing number of studies have recently explored the relationship between [[intelligence]] and [[personality]], in particular the [[Big Five personality traits]].

==General relationship==
Intelligence and personality have some common features; for example, they both follow a relatively stable pattern throughout the whole of one's life, and are to some degree genetically determined.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Caspi |first1=A. |title=The child is father of the man: personality continuities from childhood to adulthood |journal= Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=78 |issue=1 |date=2000|pages=158–172 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.78.1.158|pmid=10653512 |citeseerx=10.1.1.589.3665 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Deary |first1= I. J. |last2=Whalley |first2= L. J.|last3=Lemmon |first3= H. |last4=Crawford |first4= J. R. |last5= Starr |first5= J. M. |date=2000 |title=The stability of individual differences in mental ability from childhood to old age: follow-up of the 1932 Scottish Mental Survey|journal=Intelligence |volume=28|issue=1|pages=49–55 |doi=10.1016/s0160-2896(99)00031-8}}</ref> In addition, they are both significant predictors of various outcomes, such as educational achievement, occupational performance, and health.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Barrick  |first1=M. R.  |last2= Mount |first2= M. K. |date=  1991  |title= The Big Five personality dimensions and job performance: A meta - analysis |journal= Personnel Psychology |volume=44 |pages=1–26 |doi=10.1111/j.1744-6570.1991.tb00688.x|s2cid=144689146 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Calvin |first1= C. |last2= Batty|first2=G. D. |last3=Deary |first3=I. J. |date= 2011|title= Cognitive epidemiology: Concepts, evidence, and future directions. |publisher=Oxford:Wiley–Blackwell}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Kern|first1=M.|last2=Friedman|first2=H.|date=2011|title=Personality and health|publisher=Oxford:Wiley–Blackwell}}</ref><ref name=" Poropat2009">{{cite journal|last1=Poropat |first1= A. |date=2009|title=A meta-analysis of the five-factor model of personality and academic performance|journal=Psychological Bulletin  |volume=135|issue= 2 |pages=322–338 |doi=10.1037/a0014996|pmid= 19254083 |hdl=10072/30324|hdl-access=free}}</ref> The traditional view in psychology, which was that personality and intelligence should be studied as strictly separate entities, has come under scrutiny in light of modern personality research.<ref>{{cite magazine |last=Kaufman |first=S. B. |date=April 21, 2014 |title=How Does IQ Relate to Personality? |url=https://blogs.scientificamerican.com/beautiful-minds/how-does-iq-relate-to-personality/ |magazine=Scientific American}}</ref>

Historically, psychologists have drawn a hard distinction between intelligence and personality, arguing that intelligence is a cognitive trait while personality is non-cognitive. However, modern psychologists argue that intelligence and personality are intertwined, noting that personality traits tend to be related to specific cognitive patterns. For example, [[neuroticism]] is a personality trait that is related to rumination and compulsive thinking about possible threats.<ref>{{cite journal   |last1=Nolan|first1=S. A. |last2= Roberts|first2= J. E.|last3= Gotlib|first3= I. H.|date= 1998|title= Neuroticism and ruminative response style as predictors of change in depressive symptomatology|journal=Cognitive Therapy and Research|volume=22|issue=5 |pages=445–455|doi=10.1023/a:1018769531641|s2cid=15419457 }}</ref> Similarly, agreeableness is a personality trait that is related to the consideration of others' mental states.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Nettle|first1= D. |last2= Liddle |first2=B.|date=2008 |title=Agreeableness is related to social-cognitive, but not social-perceptual, theory of mind|journal=European Journal of Personality |volume= 22|issue= 4 |pages= 323–335 |doi=10.1002/per.672|citeseerx= 10.1.1.594.7750 |s2cid= 51682037 }}</ref> The finding that IQ predicts work performance, academic achievement, and health might also point to a link between intelligence and personality, or else be grounds for further research into their relationship.<ref>{{cite book|last1= Sternberg |first1=R. J.|last2= Grigorenko |first2= E. L.| title=The general factor of intelligence: How general is it? |pages= 331–380|location=Mahwah |publisher= NJ: Erlbaum}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Gottfredson |first1=L. S. |last2=Deary|first2= I. J. |date= 2004|title=Intelligence predicts health and longevity, but why?|journal= Current Directions in Psychological Science|volume=13|pages=1–4 |doi=10.1111/j.0963-7214.2004.01301001.x|s2cid=15176389 }}</ref>

Intelligence and personality are typically not tested in the same way. Intelligence is assessed using ability tests (such as IQ tests), whereas personality is assessed using questionnaires. It has been suggested that intelligence should reflect an individual's ''maximal performance'', while personality should reflect their ''typical behaviour''.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Cronbach|first1= L. J. |date= 1949|title=Essentials of psychological testing }}</ref>

==By personality trait==
===Openness===
[[Openness to experience|Openness]] shows the strongest positive relationship with ''[[g factor (psychometrics)|g]]'' (general intelligence) among the Big Five personality traits. The main meta-analytic estimates of correlations have ranged from .17 to .23.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=Stanek |first=Kevin |date=2014 |title=Meta-Analyses of Personality and Cognitive Ability |url=http://conservancy.umn.edu/handle/11299/201107 |journal=PhD Thesis |hdl=11299/201107 |language=en}}</ref>  Individuals with a high level of openness enjoy the experience of learning and prefer an intellectually stimulating environment. Meta-analytic research shows that openness is more strongly related to [[Fluid and crystallized intelligence|crystallized intelligence]] ({{nowrap|''r'' {{=}} .25}}) than with [[Fluid and crystallized intelligence|fluid intelligence]] ({{nowrap|''r'' {{=}} .17}}).<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=Anglim |first1=Jeromy |last2=Dunlop |first2=Patrick D. |last3=Wee |first3=Serena |last4=Horwood |first4=Sharon |last5=Wood |first5=Joshua K. |last6=Marty |first6=Andrew |date=2022 |title=Personality and intelligence: A meta-analysis. |url=https://psyarxiv.com/ar6g3/download |journal=Psychological Bulletin |language=en |volume=148 |issue=5–6 |pages=301–336 |doi=10.1037/bul0000373 |s2cid=253344751 |issn=1939-1455|hdl=20.500.11937/89668 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>  

Some psychologists have recently pointed out that previous instruments used to measure openness actually assessed two distinctive aspects. The first is intellect, which reflects intellectual engagement and perceived intelligence and is marked by ideas, while the second is emotion, which reflects the artistic and contemplative qualities related to being engaged in sensation and perception and is marked by fantasy, aesthetics, feelings and actions.<ref>{{cite journal|last1= DeYoung|first1=C. G.|last2=Quilty |first2=L. C. |last3=Peterson|first3= J. B.|date=2007|title=Between facets and domains: 10 aspects of the Big Five. |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=93|issue=5|pages=  880–896 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.93.5.880 |pmid=17983306|s2cid=8261816 }}</ref> Intelligence is more strongly related to intellectual engagement than with interest in aesthetics and fantasy.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last1=Stanek |first1=Kevin C. |last2=Ones |first2=Deniz S. |date=2023-06-06 |title=Meta-analytic relations between personality and cognitive ability |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |language=en |volume=120 |issue=23 |article-number=e2212794120 |doi=10.1073/pnas.2212794120 |issn=0027-8424 |pmc=10266031 |pmid=37252971|bibcode=2023PNAS..12012794S }}</ref> On this basis, intellect was found to be associated with the neural system of the [[working memory]], which is related to ''g'', whereas openness was not.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=DeYoung|first1= C. G. |last2= Shamosh|first2= N. A.|last3=Green|first3=A. E.|last4= Braver|first4= T. S.|last5=Gray|first5=J. R.|date= 2009 |title=Intellect as distinct from Openness: Differences revealed by fMRI of working memory. |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume= 97|issue= 5 |pages=883–892 |doi=10.1037/a0016615 |pmid=19857008 |pmc=2805551}}</ref> In addition, according to a [[Behavioural genetics|behavioral genetics]] study, intellect is genetically closer to intelligence than openness.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Wainwright|first1=M. A. |last2= Wright |first2= M. J. |last3=Luciano |first3=M.|last4=Geffen|first4=G. M. |last5= Martin |first5= N. G. |date= 2008|title= Genetic covariation among facets of openness to experience and general cognitive ability .|journal= Twin Research and Human Genetics|volume=11 |issue=3 |pages=275–286 |doi=10.1375/twin.11.3.275|pmid=18498206 |url=https://www.pure.ed.ac.uk/ws/files/11919382/2008_Genetic_covariation_among_facets_of_openness_to_experience_and_general_cognitive_ability.pdf |hdl=20.500.11820/142f022a-ee86-4c68-b327-7c73601f0e34 |s2cid=9994912 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>

=== Neuroticism ===
Neuroticism has a meaningful negative correlation with intelligence. The main large [[Meta-analysis|meta-analyses]] have obtained correlations around {{nowrap|''r'' {{=}} −.09}}.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /> Debate exists about the extent to which the correlation reflects a substantive relationship or issues with measurement. Researchers have noted that neuroticism is correlated with [[test anxiety]],<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Chamorro - Premuzic |first1=T. |last2=Ahmetoglu |first2=G. |last3=Furnham |first3=A. |date=2008 |title=Little more than personality:Trait determinants of test anxiety . |journal=Learning and Individual Differences |volume=18 |issue=2 |pages=258–263 |doi=10.1016/j.lindif.2007.09.002}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Wicherts |first1=J. M. |last2=Scholten |first2=A. |date=2010 |title=Test anxiety and the validity of cognitive tests: A confirmatory factor analysis perspective and some empirical findings. |journal=Intelligence |volume=38 |pages=169–178 |doi=10.1016/j.intell.2009.09.008}}</ref> which refers to the psychological distress experienced by individuals prior to, or during, an evaluative situation.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Zuckerman |first1=M. |title=Emotions and anxiety: New concepts, methods, and applications |last2=Spielberger |first2=C. D. |publisher=New York:Wiley |pages=57–81}}</ref> As such, some researchers have argued that neuroticism leads to test anxiety and under performance on cognitive tests. However, other researchers have argued that test anxiety is mostly incidental and caused by neuroticism, contextual pressures, and lower cognitive ability.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Sommer |first1=Markus |last2=Arendasy |first2=Martin E. |date=2014-01-01 |title=Comparing different explanations of the effect of test anxiety on respondents' test scores |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160289613001694 |journal=Intelligence |language=en |volume=42 |pages=115–127 |doi=10.1016/j.intell.2013.11.003 |issn=0160-2896|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book |last1=Stanek |first1=Kevin |url=https://umnlibraries.manifoldapp.org/projects/of-anchors-and-sails |title=Of Anchors & Sails: Personality-Ability Trait Constellations |last2=Ones |first2=Deniz |date=2023-11-20 |publisher=University of Minnesota |isbn=978-1-946135-98-8 |doi=10.24926/9781946135988}}</ref>

According to the results of a [[longitudinal study]] conducted by Gow et al., (2005), neuroticism influences an age-related decline in intelligence and there is a small negative correlation between neuroticism and a change in the level of IQ ({{nowrap|''r'' {{=}} −.18}}).<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Gow |first1=A. J. |last2=Whiteman |first2=M. C. |last3=Pattie |first3=A. |last4=Deary |first4=I. J. |date=2005 |title=The personality–intelligence interface: Insights from an ageing cohort. |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |volume=39 |issue=4 |pages=751–761 |doi=10.1016/j.paid.2005.01.028}}</ref> Although it is still debatable if neuroticism reduces general intelligence, this study provided some valuable evidence and a direction for research. 
In addition, some interaction between intelligence and neuroticism has been found. Individuals with a high level of neuroticism demonstrated a poor performance, health, and adjustment only if they had a low level of intelligence.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Leikas |first1=S. |last2=M¨akinen |first2=S. |last3=L¨ onnqvist |first3=J.E. |last4=Verkasalo |first4=M. |date=2009 |title=Cognitive ability×Emotional stability interactions on adjustment. |journal=European Journal of Personality |volume=23 |issue=4 |pages=329–342 |doi=10.1002/per.711 |s2cid=145265142}}</ref> Therefore, intelligence may act as a buffer against the negative effects of neuroticism in individuals.

===Conscientiousness===
The association between [[conscientiousness]] and intelligence is complex and uncertain. Some researchers theorize that people with lower levels of intelligence compensate for their lower level of cognitive ability by being more structured and effortful.<ref name="Chamorro-Premuzic2005">{{cite journal|last1= Chamorro-Premuzic|first1=T.|last2=Moutafi|first2=J.|last3=Furnham|first3=A.|date= 2005|title=The relationship between personality traits, subjectively-assessed and fluid intelligence.|journal=Personality and Individual Differences |volume=38|issue=7 |pages=1517–1528|doi=10.1016/j.paid.2004.09.018}}</ref> While some studies have obtained negative correlations,<ref name="Poropat2009" /><ref name="Chamorro-Premuzic2005" /><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Moutafi|first1=J.|last2=Furnham|first2=A.|last3=Crump|first3=J.|date= 2003|title=Demographic and personality predictors of intelligence: A study using the NEO Personality Inventory and the Myers-Briggs Type Indicator. |journal=European Journal of Personality|volume=17|pages=79–94|doi=10.1002/per.471|s2cid=143599504}}</ref><ref name="Judge2007">{{cite journal|last1= Judge|first1=T. A.|last2=Jackson|first2=C. L.|last3=Shaw|first3= J. C.|last4=Scott |first4=B. A.|last5=Rich|first5=B. L. |date=2007 |title=Self-efficacy and work-related performance: the integral role of individual differences |journal=Journal of Applied Psychology|volume=92|issue=1 |pages=107–127 |doi=10.1037/0021-9010.92.1.107 |pmid=17227155}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1= Moutafi|first1=J.|last2=Furnham|first2=A.|last3=Crump|first3=J.|date= 2006|title= What facets of openness and conscientiousness predict fluid intelligence score? |journal=Learning and Individual Differences|volume=16 |pages=31–42|doi=10.1016/j.lindif.2005.06.003}}</ref> others have not.<ref name="Austin2002">{{cite journal |last1=Austin= |first1=A. J. |last2=Deary |first2=I. J. |last3=Whiteman |first3=M. C. |last4=Fowkes |first4=F. G. R. |last5=Padersen |first5=N. L. |last6=Rabbitt |first6=P. |last7=Bent |first7=N. |last8=McInnes |first8=L. |date=2002 |title=Relationships between ability and personality: Does intelligence contribute positively to personal and social adjustment? |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |volume=32 |issue=8 |pages=1391–1411 |doi=10.1016/s0191-8869(01)00129-5}}</ref><ref name="Ackerman1997">{{cite journal |last1=Ackerman |first1=P. L. |last2=Heggestad |first2=E. D. |date=1997 |title=Intelligence, personality, and interests: Evidence for overlapping traits. |journal=Psychological Bulletin |volume=121 |issue=2 |pages=219–245 |doi=10.1037/0033-2909.121.2.219 |pmid=9100487}}</ref> In particular, the largest current meta-analysis of 369 studies ({{nowrap|''n'' {{=}} 345,165}}) obtained a correlation of {{nowrap|''r'' {{=}} .01}} though numerous aspect and facet level relations emerged, especially with sub-dimensions of intelligence (e.g., .32 for the industriousness aspect with general mental ability, .20 for order facet with [[visual processing]], .39 for dependability with social studies knowledge).<ref name=":2" />

Furthermore, some interaction has been found between conscientiousness and intelligence. Conscientiousness has been found to be a stronger predictor of safety behaviour in individuals with a low level of intelligence than in those with a high level.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Postlethwaite|first1= B.|last2= Robbins|first2=S.|last3= Rickerson|first3=  J.|last4=McKinniss|first4= T.|date=2009|title= The moderation of conscientiousness by cognitive ability when predicting workplace safety behaviour.|journal=Personality and Individual Differences |volume=  47 |issue= 7|pages= 711–716|doi=10.1016/j.paid.2009.06.008}}</ref> This interaction may also be found in educational and occupational settings in future studies. Therefore, relatively speaking, an increase in either conscientiousness or intelligence may compensate for a deficiency in the other.

===Extraversion===
The largest current meta-analyses have found few correlations between overall extraversion and intelligence (e.g., corrected r = -.02 with general mental ability based on 502 effect sizes and 393,929 people).<ref name=":2" /> However, within extraversion are numerous aspects and facets. For example, the activity facet, which displayed positive, meaningful relations with memory, visual processing, and processing speed abilities (e.g., .25 with ideational fluency) as well as the spectrum of acquired knowledge constructs (e.g., .28 with general verbal information).<ref name=":2" /> 

There are some moderating variables in the relationship between extraversion and ''g'' including differences in the assessment instruments and samples' age and sensory stimulation; for example, no meaningful correlation was found between extraversion and intelligence in the samples of children.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Rawlings |first1=D. |last2=Skok|first2=M.|date=1993|title= Extraversion, venturesomeness and intelligence in children.|journal= Personality and Individual Differences|volume=15|issue=4 |pages=389–396|doi=10.1016/0191-8869(93)90066-c}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1= Saklofske|first1=D. H.|last2= Kostura|first2= D. D.|date=1990|title=   Extraversion–introversion and intelligence.|journal=Personality and Individual Differences|volume=11 |issue=6|pages=547–551|doi=10.1016/0191-8869(90)90036-q}}</ref> Furthermore, Bates and Rock (2004) used [[Raven's Progressive Matrices|Raven's matrices]] and found that extraverts performed better than introverts with increasing auditory stimulation,<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Bates|first1=T. C.|last2=Rock|first2=A.|date=2004|title=Personality and information processing speed: Independent influences on intelligent performance. |journal=Intelligence|volume=32|issue=1|pages=33–46|doi=10.1016/j.intell.2003.08.002}}</ref> whereas introverts performed best in silence. This result is consistent with that of Revelle et al. (1976).<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Revelle|first1=W.|last2=Amaral|first2=P.|last3=Turriff |first3=S. |date= 1976|title= Introversion/extraversion, time stress, and caffeine|journal=Science |volume= 192 |issue=4235|pages= 149–150 |doi=10.1126/science.1257762 |pmid=1257762}}</ref> 

===Agreeableness===
Meta-analytic research suggests that [[agreeableness]] and intelligence are uncorrelated at the global level.<ref name=":2" /> However, some components of agreeableness have been found to be related to cognitive abilities. For example, aggression is negatively associated with intelligence (''r'' is around {{nowrap|−.20)}}<ref>{{cite journal|issue=4|last1=DeYoung|first1=C. G.|last2=Peterson|first2= J. B.|last3=S´eguin |first3= J. R.|last4=Pihl|first4=R. O.|last5=Tremblay|first5= R. E.|date= 2008|title=Externalizing behavior and the higher-order factors of the Big Five. |journal= Journal of Abnormal Psychology|volume= 117|pages=947–953|doi=10.1037/a0013742|pmid=19025240|s2cid=14737521}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1= Séguin|first1=J. R.|last2=Boulerice|first2= B.|last3= Harden|first3=P. |last4= Tremblay|first4=R. E.|last5=Pihl|first5=R. O.|date= 1999 |title=Executive functions and physical aggression after controlling for attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, general memory, and IQ. |journal=  Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry|volume= 40|issue=8|pages= 1197–1208 |doi=10.1111/1469-7610.00536|pmid=10604398|citeseerx=10.1.1.565.303}}</ref> because unintelligent people may experience more frustration, which may lead to aggression<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Lynam|first1=D. R.|last2=Moffitt|first2=T. E. |last3= Stouthamer-Loeber|first3=M. |date= 1993|title= Explaining the relation between IQ and delinquency: Class, race, test motivation, school failure, or self-control? |journal= Journal of Abnormal Psychology|volume= 102|issue=2|pages=187–196 |doi=10.1037/0021-843x.102.2.187|pmid=8315131}}</ref> and aggression and intelligence may share some biological factors.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=DeYoung|first1= C. G.|last2=Peterson|first2=J. B.|last3=Séguin|first3=J. R. |last4=Mejia|first4= J. M.|last5=Pihl|first5=R. O.|last6=Beitchman|first6=J. H.|last7=Jain|first7=U. |last8=Tremblay|first8=R. E.|last9=Kennedy|first9=J. L.|last10=Palmour|first10=R. M.|date=2006|title= The dopamine D4 receptor gene and moderation of the association between externalizing behavior and IQ.|journal=Archives of General Psychiatry|volume=63|issue= 12|pages=1410–1416|doi=10.1001/archpsyc.63.12.1410|pmid=17146015|pmc=3283582}}</ref> The largest meta-analyses of cognitive ability and personality trait aggression have found negligible connections.<ref name=":2" /> In addition, emotional perception and emotional facilitation, which are also components of agreeableness, have been found to be significantly correlated with intelligence.<ref>{{cite journal|last1= Mayer|first1=J. D.|last2=Salovey|first2= P.|last3= Caruso|first3= D. R. |date= 2004|title=Emotional intelligence: Theory, findings, and implications|journal=Psychological Inquiry |volume= 60 |issue=3|pages=197–215 |doi=10.1207/s15327965pli1503_02|s2cid=144415437}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1= Mayer|first1=J. D.|last2=Salovey |first2= P. |last3= Caruso |first3=D. R. |date= 2008|title=  Emotional intelligence: New ability or eclectic traits? |journal=American Psychologist|volume=63|issue=6|pages= 503–517 |doi=10.1037/0003-066x.63.6.503|pmid=18793038|citeseerx=10.1.1.453.6438}}</ref> This may be because emotional perception and emotional facilitation are components of [[emotional intelligence]] and some researchers have found that emotional intelligence is a Second-Stratum Factor of ''g''.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=MacCann|first1=C.|last2=Joseph|first2=D. L.|last3=Newman|first3= D. A.|last4=Roberts|first4=R. D.|date=2014|title= Emotional intelligence is a second-stratum factor of intelligence: Evidence from hierarchical and bifactor models.|journal=Emotion|volume=14|issue= 2|pages= 358–374|doi=10.1037/a0034755|pmid=24341786}}</ref> Similarly, meta-analysis suggests that the related trait of [[Honesty-humility factor of the HEXACO model of personality|honesty-humility]] is also uncorrelated with intelligence.<ref name=":0" /> On the other hand, the largest meta-analyses and agreeableness traits and cognitive abilities have found numerous links.<ref name=":2" /> For example, the aspects of agreeableness (i.e., compassion and politeness) have meaningful and opposite relations with cognitive abilities. For example, compassion correlates .26 with general mental ability whereas politeness correlates -.22 with general science knowledge. Facets of agreeableness also demonstrate some meaningful connections with various cognitive abilities (e.g., cooperation and processing speed correlate .20, modesty and ideational fluency correlate -.17).<ref name=":3" />

==References==
{{Reflist}}

[[Category:Intelligence]]
[[Category:Personality]]
